parts.content: State the type of bonding present in sodium chloride and in carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$: Ionic bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $CCl_4$: Covalent bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze Sodium Chloride Elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first identify the types of elements involved using the periodic table (Topic S3.1):

- **Sodium ($Na$):** Located in Group 1, it is a metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** Located in Group 17, it is a non-metal.

This classification is a prerequisite found in **Structure 3: Classification of matter**.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $NaCl$
steps.content:
According to the **Elemental Classification** method, a bond formed between a metal and a non-metal is classified as an **ionic bond**. This occurs because the metal atom transfers electrons to the non-metal atom to achieve a stable electron configuration.

$$NaCl: \text{Ionic bonding}$$

steps.title: Analyze Carbon Tetrachloride Elements
steps.content:
Next, we look at the elements in carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):

- **Carbon ($C$):** A non-metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** A non-metal.

Identifying these elements correctly is key to using the **covalent model** covered in Topic S2.2.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
When two non-metals react, they share electron pairs rather than transferring them. The bond formed between two non-metals is classified as a **covalent bond**.

$$CCl_4: \text{Covalent bonding}$$

steps.title: Use Electronegativity Difference Approach
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type, we use the electronegativity values ($\chi$) from the IB Data Booklet. The difference in electronegativity ($\Delta\chi$) between two atoms tells us if the bond is ionic or covalent.

Generally:
- **$\Delta\chi > 1.8$**: Ionic bonding
- **$\Delta\chi < 1.8$**: Covalent bonding

This concept is part of **Topic S2.4: Bonding as a continuum**.

steps.title: Bonding in Sodium Chloride
steps.content:
Using the Pauling scale in the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{Na}) = 0.9$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for sodium chloride ($NaCl$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 0.9 = 2.3$$

Since $2.3$ is significantly greater than $1.8$, the bonding is **ionic**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract 0.9 from 3.2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for NaCl.

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
Using the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{C}) = 2.6$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 2.6 = 0.6$$

Since $0.6$ is much less than $1.8$, the bonding is **covalent**.

gdc.instructions: Subtract 2.6 from 3.2.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 2.6 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 2.6 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for CCl4.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the electronegativity differences:
- **Sodium chloride:** Ionic bonding
- **Carbon tetrachloride:** Covalent bonding

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content:
The diagram shows a conductivity test involving a battery, a bulb, and electrodes. This setup allows us to deduce bonding types by observing whether a substance conducts electricity in its solid and molten (liquid) states. 

*   **Ionic compounds** conduct electricity when molten because their ions are free to move, but they do not conduct when solid because the ions are locked in a rigid lattice.
*   **Molecular covalent compounds** do not conduct in either state because they consist of neutral molecules with no free-moving charged particles (ions or electrons).

highlights.text: investigate the properties of sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride in both solid and molten states

steps.title: Deduce bonding for NaCl
steps.content:
Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is formed from a metal (Sodium) and a non-metal (Chlorine). In this experiment, $NaCl$ would cause the bulb to light up only when molten. This is because melting breaks the strong electrostatic attractions in the ionic lattice, allowing the $Na^{+}$ and $Cl^{-}$ ions to move toward the electrodes. This behavior is the classic signature of **ionic bonding**.

You can find more about this in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Deduce bonding for CCl4
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$) is composed of two non-metals (Carbon and Chlorine). These atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer shell. In the conductivity test, the bulb would not light up regardless of whether the substance is solid or liquid, because there are no ions or delocalized electrons to carry a charge. This identifies it as having **covalent bonding**.

Review **Topic S2.2: The covalent model** for more details on molecular structures.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on their physical properties and the elements involved:

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$):** Ionic bonding
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$):** Covalent bonding

parts.content: Explain why sodium chloride does not conduct electricity in the solid state but does when molten.
parts.markscheme:
- In solid $NaCl$, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In molten $NaCl$, ions are mobile and can carry charge. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the charge carriers
steps.content: To understand electrical conductivity in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first need to identify what carries the charge. $NaCl$ is an ionic compound made of sodium ions ($Na^+$) and chloride ions ($Cl^-$). For a substance to conduct electricity, it must contain particles that are both **charged** and **free to move**.

steps.title: Explain the solid state
steps.content:
In the solid state, $NaCl$ exists as a giant ionic lattice. The $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are held in fixed positions by strong electrostatic forces of attraction. 

Because the ions are **fixed in the lattice** and cannot move from place to place, they cannot carry an electric current. This is why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In solid NaCl, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move.

steps.title: Explain the molten state
steps.content:
When sodium chloride is heated until it becomes molten (liquid), the high temperature provides enough energy to overcome the strong electrostatic forces holding the lattice together. 

In this state, the lattice structure breaks down, and the **ions become mobile**. Since the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are now free to move throughout the liquid, they can carry the electric charge toward the electrodes, allowing the substance to conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In molten NaCl, ions are mobile and can carry charge.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both states:
1.  **Solid state:** Ions are in fixed positions in a lattice (1 mark).
2.  **Molten state:** Ions are free to move/mobile (1 mark).

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** in your IB Chemistry syllabus.

parts.content: Suggest why carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state.
parts.markscheme: $CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define electrical conductivity requirements
steps.content:
To understand why a substance conducts (or doesn't conduct) electricity, we use **Charge Carrier Analysis**. For any material to conduct an electric current, it must possess particles that are both **charged** and **mobile** (free to move). These charge carriers are typically either:

1.  **Delocalized electrons** (common in metals and graphite).
2.  **Free-moving ions** (common in molten or aqueous ionic compounds).

steps.title: Analyze the bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple molecular covalent** compound. In this structure, carbon and chlorine atoms are held together by localized covalent bonds where electrons are shared between specific atoms. 

This means:
- There are no ions present because the atoms are not losing or gaining electrons to form charged particles.
- There are no delocalized electrons because all valence electrons are either involved in specific bonds or localized as lone pairs on the chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Apply Charge Carrier Analysis
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ consists of neutral molecules, there are no mobile ions to carry charge. Even in the molten (liquid) state, the molecules remain neutral and the electrons remain localized within the covalent bonds. 

Because it lacks both **free-moving ions** and **delocalized electrons**, it cannot conduct electricity in any state.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state

steps.title: Final Markscheme Alignment
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you simply need to state the absence of these carriers.

**Answer:**
$CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: To understand why a substance conducts or doesn't conduct electricity, we first look at its bonding. Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple covalent molecular substance**. It is formed from two non-metals (carbon and chlorine) sharing electrons to form covalent bonds.

steps.title: Analyze the charge carriers
steps.content:
For electricity to flow, a substance must have **mobile charged particles**, such as free-moving ions or delocalized electrons. 

In $CCl_4$:
1. The substance consists of **neutral molecules**. There are no ions present in the solid or liquid (molten) state.
2. The valence electrons are **localized** within the specific covalent bonds between the carbon and chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Relate structure to conductivity
steps.content:
Because the molecules are neutral and the electrons are held tightly within the bonds, there are no charged particles available to move and carry a current. 

This is why $CCl_4$ remains a non-conductor in any state, unlike ionic compounds (like $NaCl$) which can conduct when molten because their ions become free to move.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to state that the substance lacks the necessary mobile charges.

**Final Answer:** Carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity because it has **no free-moving (mobile) ions or electrons**.

parts.content: Use the diagram and your knowledge of structure and bonding to deduce which substance(s) contain mobile ions.
parts.markscheme: Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding types
steps.content:
To determine which substance has mobile ions, we first need to look at their bonding. 

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$)** is an **ionic compound** formed between a metal (Na) and a non-metal (Cl). It consists of a giant lattice of positive sodium ions ($Na^+$) and negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$).
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$)** is a **covalent molecular compound** formed between two non-metals. It consists of neutral molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces.

This fundamental difference is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** and **Structure 2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Evaluate ion mobility
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the state of these substances. 

In **solid $NaCl$**, the ions are locked into a rigid 3D lattice by strong electrostatic forces. Because they are in fixed positions, they cannot move to carry a charge. However, when $NaCl$ is **molten** (melted), the lattice structure breaks down, and the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are free to move throughout the liquid.

In contrast, $CCl_4$ is made of neutral molecules. Whether it is solid or molten, it does not contain ions at all, only molecules.

steps.title: Deduce the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:
1. $CCl_4$ has no ions in either state.
2. Solid $NaCl$ has ions, but they are not mobile.
3. **Molten $NaCl$** has ions that are free to move.

Therefore, the only substance containing mobile ions is molten $NaCl$.

steps.title: Analyze $CCl_4$ properties
steps.content: First, let's look at carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). It is a covalent molecular substance composed of neutral molecules. Because it does not contain any ions in either its solid or molten state, we can eliminate it immediately from the list of substances containing mobile ions.

steps.title: Evaluate solid $NaCl$
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is an ionic compound containing $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. However, in its solid state, these ions are locked in a rigid 3D lattice structure. While the ions exist, they are not **mobile** because they are held firmly in place by strong electrostatic attractions.

steps.title: Identify the mobile phase
steps.content: When $NaCl$ is heated to its melting point to become molten, the giant ionic lattice breaks down. This allows the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions to move freely throughout the liquid. Therefore, only molten $NaCl$ contains ions that are actually mobile.

steps.title: Final Deduction
steps.content:
By ruling out the molecular substance ($CCl_4$) and the fixed lattice structure (solid $NaCl$), we conclude that only molten $NaCl$ fits the criteria.

**Final Answer:** Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions.

parts.content: State and explain the difference in melting point between sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$ has a much higher melting point than $CCl_4$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Due to strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice vs. weak London forces between $CCl_4$ molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: State the difference
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) has a significantly higher melting point than carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). To understand why, we need to look at how their atoms are held together in the solid state.

highlights.text: NaCl has a much higher melting point than CCl4

steps.title: Analyze sodium chloride structure
steps.content:
Sodium chloride is an ionic compound that exists as a **giant ionic lattice**. In this structure, ions are held together by **strong electrostatic attractions** between oppositely charged $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. 

Because these attractions are very strong and extend throughout the entire 3D lattice, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome them and melt the solid.

highlights.text: strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice

steps.title: Analyze carbon tetrachloride structure
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride is a **simple molecular** substance. While the $C-Cl$ bonds within the molecule are strong covalent bonds, the forces holding the $CCl_4$ molecules to each other in a solid are **weak London dispersion forces** (intermolecular forces).

Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, these London forces are the only attractions present between molecules.

highlights.text: weak London forces between CCl4 molecules

steps.title: Contrast the energy required
steps.content:
The difference in melting point comes down to the energy needed to disrupt these different types of forces:

1.  In **$NaCl$**, you must break strong ionic bonds within a giant lattice.
2.  In **$CCl_4$**, you only need to overcome weak intermolecular forces between molecules.

Therefore, $NaCl$ requires much more energy to melt, resulting in a much higher melting point. This comparison is a core concept in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

parts.content: Explain why carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature. Justify your answer based on its intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze molecular polarity
steps.content:
To determine the intermolecular forces, we first look at the structure of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). Although the individual $C-Cl$ bonds are polar due to the difference in electronegativity, the molecule has a **symmetrical tetrahedral shape**. 

Because of this symmetry, the bond dipoles cancel each other out, making the $CCl_4$ molecule **non-polar**. This is a key prerequisite concept from **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, the only type of intermolecular forces it can form are **London dispersion forces** (also known as instantaneous induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

These forces occur because of the constant movement of electrons, which creates temporary dipoles that induce dipoles in neighboring molecules.

steps.title: Explain the liquid state
steps.content:
At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the molecules have a certain amount of kinetic energy. For $CCl_4$, this energy is **insufficient to completely overcome** the London dispersion forces between the molecules. 

Because the forces are strong enough to keep the molecules close together but weak enough to allow them to slide past each other, the substance exists as a **liquid** rather than a gas. 

**Markscheme Note:** 
- Mentioning **London dispersion forces** earns the first mark.
- Stating that the molecules are **non-polar** or that there is **insufficient energy to overcome these forces** at room temperature earns the second mark.

steps.title: Identify the intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a non-polar molecule because of its symmetrical tetrahedral shape. Because it is non-polar, the only intermolecular forces acting between its molecules are weak **London dispersion forces** (also known as van der Waals forces or induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

This is the first key point needed to earn marks according to the markscheme.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature

steps.title: Apply energy-state relationship
steps.content:
The state of matter is determined by the balance between the thermal (kinetic) energy of the particles and the strength of the intermolecular forces holding them together. 

At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the average kinetic energy provided to the molecules is:
1.  **Enough** to overcome the rigid lattice structure of a solid, allowing the molecules to move and flow.
2.  **Insufficient** to completely overcome the London dispersion forces, meaning the molecules remain close together rather than flying apart as a gas.

Because the energy is enough to overcome some but not all of the forces, $CCl_4$ exists as a **liquid**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your answer should connect these two points:

*   **Mark 1:** State that $CCl_4$ molecules are held together by weak London dispersion forces.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain that at room temperature, there is insufficient energy to fully overcome these forces to form a gas, resulting in the liquid state.

This concept is covered in **Structure 2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content: To understand the properties of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$), we first look at the individual bonds. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, which creates a polar covalent bond where the electron density is pulled towards the chlorine atoms. This creates individual bond dipoles directed from $C$ to $Cl$.

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content: According to VSEPR theory (Topic S2.2.4), carbon has four bonding electron domains and no lone pairs, resulting in a **tetrahedral** geometry. Because all four atoms surrounding the central carbon are identical (chlorine), the molecule is perfectly symmetrical.

steps.title: Determine molecular polarity
steps.content: Due to the tetrahedral symmetry, the individual polar bond dipoles cancel each other out exactly. As a result, $CCl_4$ is a **non-polar molecule**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Because the molecules are non-polar, the only attractions between them are **weak London dispersion forces**. These forces arise from temporary induced dipoles. 

In the IB markscheme, identifying these forces earns the first mark.

highlights.text: Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules.

steps.title: Relate forces to state
steps.content:
At room temperature, there is insufficient thermal energy to completely overcome these intermolecular forces to turn the substance into a gas, but the forces are weak enough to allow the molecules to slide past one another. Therefore, $CCl_4$ exists as a liquid.

Stating that these forces occur between non-polar molecules earns the second mark.

highlights.text: The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis (electron dot) structure for the $Na^+$ ion and a $CCl_4$ molecule
parts.markscheme:
$Na^+$: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost) <marks>1 mark</marks>

$CCl_4$: carbon single bonded to four $Cl$ atoms; each $Cl$ with 3 lone pairs <marks>1 mark</marks>


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7e8a691d-b87a-4c65-9943-1ee90d7d96eb)

steps.title: Analyze $Na^+$ ion structure
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) is a Group 1 element with 1 valence electron. To form the $Na^+$ ion, it loses this single electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (matching Neon). 

In IB chemistry, when a metal ion loses all its valence electrons, the Lewis structure is drawn by simply showing the element's symbol and its charge. No dots are placed around it because the original valence shell is now empty.

$$Na^+$$

steps.title: Count $CCl_4$ valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw the $CCl_4$ molecule, we first calculate the total number of valence electrons available:

- Carbon ($C$) is in Group 14: **4 electrons**
- Chlorine ($Cl$) is in Group 17: **7 electrons per atom**

$$\text{Total} = 4 + (4 \times 7) = 32 \text{ electrons}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the sum of valence electrons.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the total.

calculator.description: Calculate total valence electrons for carbon tetrachloride.

steps.title: Distribute electrons in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Now we systematically distribute the 32 electrons:

1.  **Form the skeleton**: Place $C$ in the center and connect the four $Cl$ atoms with single bonds. Each bond uses 2 electrons, so $4 \times 2 = 8$ electrons used.
2.  **Complete octets**: Subtract the bonding electrons from the total ($32 - 8 = 24$). Place these 24 electrons as lone pairs around the $Cl$ atoms. Each $Cl$ needs 6 more electrons (3 lone pairs) to complete its octet.
3.  **Check Carbon**: The central Carbon already has 4 bonds (8 electrons), so its octet is satisfied.

Every atom now has a full valence shell using exactly 32 electrons.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the 8 bonding electrons.

ti84.instructions: Subtract bonding electrons from total.

desmos.instructions: Calculate remaining electrons.

calculator.description: Verify remaining electrons for lone pairs.

steps.title: Final Lewis Structures
steps.content:
Combining the steps, your final diagrams should look like this:

- **$Na^+$**: Just the symbol and the charge: $$Na^+$$
- **$CCl_4$**: A central $C$ with four single bonds to $Cl$, and each $Cl$ surrounded by 6 dots (3 lone pairs).

$$\begin{matrix} & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \\ & & | & & \\ :\ddot{Cl} & - & C & - & \ddot{Cl}: \\ & & | & & \\ & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: Na+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)
CCl_4: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms; each Cl with 3 lone pairs

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw Lewis structures, we first determine the number of valence electrons for each atom using their group numbers in the periodic table:

*   **Sodium (Na)**: Group 1, so it has 1 valence electron.
*   **Carbon (C)**: Group 14, so it has 4 valence electrons.
*   **Chlorine (Cl)**: Group 17, so it has 7 valence electrons.

This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Draw the Sodium Ion
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) forms an ion by losing its single valence electron to achieve a stable, full inner shell. 

In a Lewis structure for a positive ion (cation), we show the symbol and the charge. Since the original third-shell electron is gone, the diagram shows no dots or crosses around the symbol.

$$[Na]^+$$

highlights.text: Na^+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
For $CCl_4$, Carbon is the central atom. It uses its 4 valence electrons (represented as crosses $\times$) to form four single covalent bonds with four Chlorine atoms.

Each Chlorine atom provides 1 electron (represented as a dot $\bullet$) to share with Carbon, creating four bonding pairs. This allows Carbon to reach a stable octet (8 electrons).

$$\begin{matrix} & \text{Cl} & \\ \text{Cl} & \text{C} & \text{Cl} \\ & \text{Cl} & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms

steps.title: Complete Chlorine's lone pairs
steps.content:
Each Chlorine atom starts with 7 valence electrons. Since it shares only 1 electron with Carbon, it has 6 non-bonding electrons remaining. These must be drawn as **3 lone pairs** (pairs of dots $\bullet \bullet$) around each Chlorine atom.

This ensures every Chlorine atom also satisfies the octet rule ($2$ shared $+ 6$ non-bonding $= 8$ electrons).

**Final Dot and Cross Representation:**
$$\begin{array}{ccc} & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \times \bullet & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet & \bullet \text{C} \times & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \bullet \times & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \end{array}$$

parts.content: State what the energy value of $0\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ represents for the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ ion.
parts.markscheme: The energy of $0\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ represents the electron being completely removed from the atom; the electron and nucleus are infinitely separated. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Understand the energy scale
steps.content: In atomic physics, we use a scale where energy levels are negative. This indicates that the electron is "trapped" or bound by the electrostatic attraction of the nucleus. As the energy level $n$ increases, the energy values become less negative, moving closer to zero.

steps.title: Define the zero-energy limit
steps.content: The value $0\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ represents the limit as the principal quantum number $n$ approaches infinity ($n = \infty$). At this point, the electron has gained exactly enough energy to overcome the pull of the nucleus completely.

steps.title: Identify the physical state
steps.content:
When the energy reaches $0\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$, the electron is no longer bound to the nucleus. This represents the **ionization** of the ion. In this state, the electron and the nucleus are considered to be **infinitely separated**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** in your syllabus, specifically regarding how emission spectra relate to discrete energy levels and the eventual removal of an electron.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: To earn the mark, you should state that the energy represents the electron being **completely removed** from the ion or that the electron and nucleus are **infinitely separated**.

parts.content:
Calculate the wavelength, in nm, of the photon emitted when an electron falls from $n=4$ to $n=2$.
(Planck’s constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \mathrm{J\ s}$, speed of light $c = 3.00 \times 10^8\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}$)
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate energy difference:
  $\Delta E = (-330) - (-1310) = 980\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Convert to J per particle:
  $E = 980\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}} \times \frac{1000\ \mathrm{J}}{1\ \mathrm{kJ}} \times \frac{1}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 1.63 \times 10^{-18}\ \mathrm{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Use $E = hc/\lambda$ to find wavelength:
  $\lambda = \frac{hc}{E} = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)}{1.63 \times 10^{-18}} = 1.22 \times 10^{-7}\ \mathrm{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Convert to nm:
  $\lambda = 122\ \mathrm{nm}$

steps.title: Calculate Energy Change
steps.content:
First, we determine the energy released when the electron falls from $n=4$ to $n=2$ by finding the difference between these two energy levels. According to the diagram:
- Energy at $n=4$: $-330\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$
- Energy at $n=2$: $-1310\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{final}} - E_{\text{initial}}$$
$$\Delta E = -1310 - (-330) = -980\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The magnitude of energy released is $980\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

highlights.text: electron falls from n=4 to n=2

steps.title: Convert to Joules per Photon
steps.content:
The value $980\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ represents the energy for one mole of photons. To find the energy of a single photon ($E$), we need to convert kilojoules to Joules and divide by the **Avogadro constant** ($N_A$).

1. Convert kJ to J: $980 \times 1000 = 980,000\text{ J mol}^{-1}$
2. Divide by $N_A$ (from the Data Booklet):

$$E = \frac{980,000\text{ J mol}^{-1}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}\text{ mol}^{-1}}$$

$$E \approx 1.63 \times 10^{-18}\text{ J}$$

steps.title: Solve for Wavelength
steps.content:
Now we use the relationship between energy and wavelength. From the Data Booklet, we have $E = hf$ and $c = f\lambda$. Combining these gives:

$$E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \implies \lambda = \frac{hc}{E}$$

Substitute the constants:
- $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J s}$
- $c = 3.00 \times 10^8\text{ m s}^{-1}$

$$\lambda = \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8)}{1.63 \times 10^{-18}}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template and scientific notation to evaluate the wavelength.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen. Use the [EE] key (2nd + ,) for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula into the input bar to find the result in scientific notation.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength in meters using the photon energy equation.

steps.title: Convert to Nanometers
steps.content:
The calculation gives $\lambda \approx 1.22 \times 10^{-7}\text{ m}$. To convert this to nanometers ($1\text{ nm} = 10^{-9}\text{ m}$), we multiply by $10^9$:

$$\lambda = 1.22 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^9$$
$$\lambda = 122\text{ nm}$$

This wavelength corresponds to a line in the ultraviolet region of the spectrum.

steps.title: Calculate molar energy difference
steps.content:
First, we determine the energy change ($ΔE$) when the electron falls from $n=4$ to $n=2$. According to the energy level diagram:
- Energy at $n=4$: $-330\ \text{kJ\ mol}^{-1}$
- Energy at $n=2$: $-1310\ \text{kJ\ mol}^{-1}$

The energy of the emitted photon corresponds to the difference between these levels:

$$\Delta E_{\text{molar}} = |(-1310) - (-330)| = 980\ \text{kJ\ mol}^{-1}$$

This concept is a core part of **S1.3.1 Emission spectra and energy levels** in your IB syllabus.

steps.title: Derive the combined equation
steps.content:
To find the wavelength ($\lambda$) in one step, we combine two fundamental physics equations from your data booklet. 

1. The energy of a single photon: 
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

2. The relationship between molar energy and single particle energy using the Avogadro constant ($L$):
$$E_{\text{photon}} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{molar}} \times 10^3}{L}$$
*(Note: We multiply by $10^3$ to convert kJ to Joules)*.

Setting these equal and solving for $\lambda$ gives our combined algebraic expression:

$$\lambda = \frac{h \cdot c \cdot L}{\Delta E_{\text{molar}} \times 10^3}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known constants and our calculated energy difference into the combined expression. 

Constants from the data booklet:
- $$h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J s}$$
- $$c = 3.00 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$
- $$L = 6.02 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \frac{(6.63 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3.00 \times 10^8) \times (6.02 \times 10^{23})}{980 \times 10^3} \\ \lambda &\approx 1.22 × 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of the combined expression.

ti84.instructions: Enter the following expression into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Input the fraction into the calculator to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength in meters using the combined formula.

steps.title: Convert to nanometers
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the result from meters to nanometers ($1\ \text{m} = 10^9\ \text{nm}$):

$$\lambda = 1.22 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m} \times 10^9\ \text{nm/m} = 122\ \text{nm}$$

The photon emitted has a wavelength of **122 nm** (rounded to 3 significant figures).

parts.content: Determine the first ionization energy of the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ ion in $\mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identification of ionization as transition from $n=1$ to $n=\infty$ (where $E=0$): <marks>M1</marks>
  $IE = E_{\infty} - E_1 = 0 - (-5250)$

- Final answer:
  $5250\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the ionization transition
steps.content:
To find the first ionization energy, we need to understand what happens to the electron. Ionization occurs when an electron absorbs enough energy to move from its lowest energy level, or **ground state**, to a distance so far from the nucleus that it is no longer attracted to it. 

In terms of energy levels, this corresponds to a transition from the level $n=1$ to the level $n = \infty$, where the energy is defined as $0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$. This concept is part of **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

highlights.text: Determine the first ionization energy of the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ ion in $\mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$

steps.title: Identify energy values
steps.content:
Looking at the provided energy level diagram for the $\text{He}^{+}$ ion, we can identify the specific energy values for our two levels:

*   **Ground state ($n=1$):** The energy level at the bottom of the diagram is $E_1 = -5250 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.
*   **Ionization limit ($n=\infty$):** The level where the electron is free is $E_{\infty} = 0 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$.

steps.title: Calculate the ionization energy
steps.content:
The ionization energy ($IE$) is the difference in energy between the final state (free electron) and the initial state (ground state). We use the formula:

$$\text{IE} = E_{\infty} - E_1$$

Substituting the values from the diagram:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{IE} &= 0 - (-5250) \\ \text{IE} &= 5250 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn one mark for identifying the transition from $n=1$ to $n=\infty$ and one mark for the correct final numerical value.

parts.content: Compare the energy required for the electron transitions from $n=2$ to $n=3$ for the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ ion and the H atom.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ has a greater nuclear charge ($+2$) compared to H ($+1$). <marks>A1</marks>
- Therefore, transitions in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ require more energy than in H. <marks>A1</marks>
- Because the electron is more strongly attracted to the nucleus. <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Compare nuclear charges
steps.content:
To understand the difference in energy levels, we first look at the nucleus of each species. 

Hydrogen ($H$) has an atomic number of 1, meaning its nucleus contains **1 proton** (a charge of $+1$). 

Helium ($He$) has an atomic number of 2. Since we are looking at the $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ ion, it has **2 protons** (a charge of $+2$) and only 1 electron, making it electronically similar to Hydrogen but with a different "core."

highlights.text:
$
He^{+}$ has a greater nuclear charge ($+2$) compared to H ($+1$)

steps.title: Analyze electrostatic attraction
steps.content:
The electron in both species is held in orbit by the electrostatic force of attraction between the negative electron and the positive nucleus. 

Because the nucleus of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ has twice the positive charge of the $H$ nucleus, it exerts a much **stronger pull** on its electron. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom**.

highlights.text: Because the electron is more strongly attracted to the nucleus.

steps.title: Relate attraction to energy
steps.content:
A stronger electrostatic attraction means that the energy levels are "pulled" closer to the nucleus and are more widely spaced in terms of energy. 

To move an electron from $n=2$ to $n=3$, you must overcome the attraction of the nucleus. Since the electron in $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ is more strongly attracted to its $+2$ nucleus, it requires significantly **more energy** to make that jump compared to the electron in a neutral $H$ atom.

highlights.text:
transitions in $
He^{+}$ require more energy than in H.

steps.title: Compare nuclear charges
steps.content: First, we identify the atomic number ($Z$) for both species. The hydrogen atom (H) has a nuclear charge of $+1$ (it has 1 proton), while the helium ion ($\text{He}^{+}$) has a nuclear charge of $+2$ (it has 2 protons). Both species have only one electron, making them "hydrogen-like" ions.

steps.title: Analyze electrostatic attraction
steps.content: In $\text{He}^{+}$, the single electron experiences a stronger electrostatic attraction toward the nucleus compared to the electron in the H atom. This is because the $+2$ charge in the helium nucleus exerts a greater pull than the $+1$ charge in the hydrogen nucleus.

steps.title: Apply energy scaling relationship
steps.content:
For single-electron species, the energy of a level ($E_n$) scales with the square of the nuclear charge ($Z^2$). The relationship is:

$$E_n ∝ -\frac{Z^2}{n^2}$$

Because $Z$ is larger for $\text{He}^{+}$, all its energy levels are more negative (lower) and the gaps between the levels are significantly larger compared to H.

steps.title: Compare the energy transitions
steps.content:
Since the energy levels in $\text{He}^{+}$ are spread further apart due to the greater nuclear charge, the energy difference ($\Delta E$) for the transition from $n=2$ to $n=3$ is much larger in $\text{He}^{+}$ than in the H atom.

In fact, because $\Delta E ∝ Z^2$, the energy required for this transition in $\text{He}^{+}$ is approximately 4 times greater ($2^2 = 4$) than in the H atom.

gdc.instructions: Square the nuclear charge of Helium to find the scaling factor relative to Hydrogen.

ti84.instructions: Square the nuclear charge of Helium to find the scaling factor relative to Hydrogen.

calculator.description: Calculate the scaling factor between He+ (Z=2) and H (Z=1).

steps.title: Summarize the conclusion
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you mention:
1. $\text{He}^{+}$ has a higher nuclear charge ($+2$) than H ($+1$).
2. The electron is more strongly attracted to the nucleus in $\text{He}^{+}$.
3. Consequently, the transition from $n=2$ to $n=3$ requires more energy in $\text{He}^{+}$ than in H.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

parts.content: Sketch a diagram showing the emission spectrum for $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ corresponding to transitions ending at $n=1$ (Lyman series), indicating the trend in line spacing.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch lines getting closer together toward the higher energy (higher frequency) side. <marks>A1</marks>
- Show multiple lines converging at a limit. <marks>A1</marks>
- Label axis as frequency or energy. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the transitions
steps.content:
To sketch the emission spectrum, we first need to identify the transitions in the **Lyman series**. This series occurs when electrons drop from higher energy levels ($n = 2, 3, 4, \dots$) down to the ground state ($n = 1$).

The energy of the emitted photon is equal to the difference in energy between these levels:

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{high}} - E_{\text{low}}$$

This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic 1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Analyze the energy gaps
steps.content:
Looking at the energy level diagram, notice that as $n$ increases, the energy levels get closer together. Let's compare the energy differences for the first few transitions:

1.  **Transition $n=2 \to 1$**: This has the lowest energy in the series.
2.  **Transition $n=3 \to 1$**: This has more energy than $2 \to 1$.
3.  **Transition $n=4 \to 1$**: This has even more energy, but the *increase* in energy from the previous transition is smaller.

Because the gaps between $n=2, 3, 4, \dots$ shrink as $n$ approaches infinity, the differences between the resulting emission lines also shrink.

steps.title: Relate energy to frequency
steps.content:
The question asks for a spectrum, which is usually plotted against frequency ($f$) or energy ($E$). From the data booklet, we use the relationship:

$$E = hf$$

Since energy and frequency are directly proportional, the lines in the spectrum will follow the same pattern as the energy differences: as we move to higher frequencies, the lines will appear closer and closer together.

steps.title: Sketch the spectrum
steps.content:
When sketching your diagram, ensure you include these three key features to earn full marks:

1.  **Label the x-axis**: Mark it as "frequency" or "energy" (with an arrow pointing to the right for increasing values).
2.  **Draw the lines**: Start with a wide gap between the first two lines (on the left) and make the spacing progressively smaller as you move right.
3.  **Show convergence**: Draw several lines at the high-frequency end that look like they are merging together at a "convergence limit."

$$\text{Frequency (or Energy)} \longrightarrow$$
$$| \quad \quad | \quad \quad | \quad | \ ||||$$

steps.title: Define the axes
steps.content:
To sketch an emission spectrum, we first need to define our horizontal axis. In IB Chemistry, this axis represents either **energy** ($E$) or **frequency** ($f$). 

Because the energy of a photon is directly proportional to its frequency, as shown in the data booklet formula:

$$E = h f$$

Both energy and frequency increase in the same direction on your sketch (usually from left to right).

steps.title: Establish the convergence limit
steps.content:
Using the **Convergence Limit Approach**, we begin by identifying the point where the spectral lines become indistinguishable. 

In the $\text{He}^{+}$ ion, as the electron falls from higher and higher energy levels (like $n=10, n=50, n=100$) down to $n=1$, the energy differences become nearly identical. At the limit where $n = \infty$, we reach the **convergence limit**. 

On your diagram, mark a point or a line on the far right side to represent this high-energy/high-frequency limit.

steps.title: Sketch the spectral lines
steps.content:
Now, add the individual emission lines by moving away from the limit toward the lower energy (lower frequency) side:

1. Start near your convergence limit and draw several vertical lines very close together.
2. As you move to the left (toward lower frequency), increase the spacing between each subsequent line.
3. The furthest line to the left represents the lowest energy transition in the Lyman series ($n=2$ to $n=1$), so it should have the largest gap from the next line.

This trend occurs because the energy gaps between the shells ($n=1, n=2, n=3...$) get smaller as $n$ increases.

steps.title: Label the final diagram
steps.content:
To ensure you get all 3 marks, your final sketch should look like a series of vertical bars that "bunch up" on one side. Make sure to:

*   Label the x-axis as **Frequency** or **Energy**.
*   Indicate the **convergence limit** at the high-frequency end.
*   Check that your lines clearly show **increasing spacing** toward the low-frequency side.

$$\text{Low } f \xleftarrow{\quad \text{Increasing spacing} \quad} | \quad | \quad |||| \text{ Convergence Limit (High } f)$$

This question relates to **S1.3.1 Emission spectra** in your syllabus. Understanding that lines converge at high energy is essential for calculating ionization energies later!

parts.content: Discuss why the emission lines converge at high frequencies, and how this convergence relates to ionization.
parts.markscheme:
- Convergence occurs because the energy difference between adjacent levels becomes smaller as $n$ increases. <marks>R1</marks>
- At the convergence limit, the electron is ionized (no longer bound to the nucleus). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Observe energy level spacing
steps.content:
Looking at the energy level diagram for $\mathrm{He}^{+}$, notice that as the principal quantum number $n$ increases (moving from $n=1$ toward $n=6$ and beyond), the levels get closer and closer together. This decrease in the gap between adjacent levels is a fundamental property of atomic energy levels. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic 1.3: Electron configurations**. If you need to refresh your memory on how spectra are formed, review the sections on emission spectra and energy levels.

steps.title: Relate energy to frequency
steps.content:
When an electron transitions from a higher energy level to a lower one, it emits energy as a photon. The frequency ($f$) of this light is directly proportional to the energy difference between the two levels ($E$). We can see this relationship in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of the data booklet:

$$E=h f$$

Because the energy gaps between levels become smaller as $n$ increases, the differences in frequency between these transitions also become smaller. This results in the emission lines "bunching up" at high frequencies.

steps.title: Identify the convergence limit
steps.content: As $n$ continues to increase toward infinity ($n = \infty$), the energy levels eventually meet at a point called the **convergence limit**. At this specific limit, the electron is no longer bound to the nucleus by electrostatic attraction. Therefore, the frequency at this limit tells us the energy required for **ionization**, which is the complete removal of the electron from the ion.

highlights.text: emission lines converge at high frequencies

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks in an IB exam for this discussion, ensure you mention two key points:

1.  **Convergence of levels**: The energy difference between adjacent levels becomes smaller as $n$ increases, which is why the lines converge at high frequencies.
2.  **Ionization**: At the convergence limit, the electron is no longer bound to the nucleus (it has been ionized).

This qualitative understanding is essential for interpreting atomic emission spectra correctly.

steps.title: Identify the element
steps.content:
The question refers to two isotopes: ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$ and ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$. The symbol $\text{Cl}$ identifies the element as **Chlorine**. 

In IB Chemistry, you'll learn in **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom** that an element is defined by the number of protons in its nucleus.

highlights.text: State the number of protons in both isotopes.

steps.title: Find the atomic number
steps.content:
To determine the number of protons, locate Chlorine ($\text{Cl}$) in the Periodic Table. 

The **atomic number** ($Z$) is the whole number shown for each element. For Chlorine, this number is **17**. This value represents the number of protons in every atom of that element.

steps.title: Apply to both isotopes
steps.content:
Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons. 

Because both ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$ and ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$ are isotopes of Chlorine, they both must have **17 protons**.

highlights.text: State that both isotopes have 17 protons

parts.content: Deduce the number of neutrons in each isotope.
parts.markscheme:
- Deduce 18 neutrons for ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$ ($35 - 17 = 18$) <marks>A1</marks>
- Deduce 20 neutrons for ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$ ($37 - 17 = 20$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify chlorine's atomic number
steps.content:
To find the number of neutrons, we first need to identify the atomic number ($Z$) for chlorine. By looking at the Periodic Table in your data booklet, you can see that chlorine is the 17th element.

$$\text{Atomic number (Z) of Cl} = 17$$

steps.title: Use the neutron formula
steps.content:
The mass number ($A$) of an isotope is the sum of its protons and neutrons. Since the atomic number ($Z$) represents the number of protons, we can find the number of neutrons ($N$) using the subtraction approach:

$$N = A - Z$$

steps.title: Calculate neutrons for Cl-35
steps.content:
For the first isotope, ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$, the mass number is 35. Subtracting the atomic number (17) gives us the number of neutrons:

$$\begin{aligned} N &= 35 - 17 \\ N &= 18 \end{aligned}$$

This earns you the first mark.

steps.title: Calculate neutrons for Cl-37
steps.content:
For the second isotope, ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$, the mass number is 37. We use the same atomic number (17) because they are both chlorine:

$$\begin{aligned} N &= 37 - 17 \\ N &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

This earns you the second mark.

- Recognize that both isotopes have the same number of electrons and the same electron configuration <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Define Chemical Property Basis
steps.content: In chemistry, the behavior of an atom during a reaction is determined by its electrons. Specifically, chemical properties depend on the **number and arrangement of electrons** (the electron configuration) in an atom.

highlights.text: chemical properties depend on the number and arrangement of electrons

steps.title: Analyze Isotope Electrons
steps.content:
Isotopes like ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$ and ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$ are atoms of the same element. Because they have the same atomic number ($Z = 17$), they both possess the same number of protons and, therefore, the **same number of electrons**.

Since they have the same number of electrons, their **electron configuration** is identical: 

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5$$

highlights.text: both isotopes have the same number of electrons and the same electron configuration

steps.title: Link Electrons to Reactivity
steps.content: Because chemical reactivity is a function of the electron arrangement, and these isotopes have identical configurations, their chemical properties remain almost identical. The difference in the number of neutrons only affects physical properties (like mass and density), not how the atoms bond or react.

steps.title: Identify protons and atomic number
steps.content: Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different mass numbers. Since both ${}^{35}\text{Cl}$ and ${}^{37}\text{Cl}$ are isotopes of chlorine, they have the same **atomic number** ($Z = 17$). This means they both contain exactly 17 protons in their nucleus. For more on this, you can review **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom**.

steps.title: Deduce the electron configuration
steps.content:
In a neutral atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of protons. Because these isotopes have the same number of protons, they must also have the **same number of electrons** and the **same electron configuration**.

$$\text{Cl configuration: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5$$

highlights.text: same number of electrons and the same electron configuration

steps.title: Link electrons to properties
steps.content: Chemical properties are determined by the **number and arrangement of electrons**, especially the valence (outer shell) electrons. Since both isotopes have the exact same arrangement of electrons, they interact with other atoms in identical ways during chemical reactions.

highlights.text: chemical properties depend on the number and arrangement of electrons

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: In summary, while the isotopes differ in their number of neutrons (and thus their physical properties like mass), their **identical electron configurations** result in almost identical chemical properties.

steps.title: Contrast mass and electrons
steps.content: To understand why isotopes behave the same way chemically, we must contrast their physical differences with their electronic similarities. While $^{35}\text{Cl}$ and $^{37}\text{Cl}$ differ in their mass number and number of neutrons, these factors only affect physical properties (like density or boiling point), not chemical reactivity.

steps.title: Link chemistry to electrons
steps.content: In chemistry, the behavior of an atom during a reaction is determined by how it interacts with other atoms. This interaction is entirely dependent on the **number and arrangement of electrons** (the electron configuration).

highlights.text: chemical properties depend on the number and arrangement of electrons

steps.title: Identify shared electronic structure
steps.content:
Since both $^{35}\text{Cl}$ and $^{37}\text{Cl}$ are isotopes of the same element, they have the same atomic number ($Z = 17$). This means they both have 17 protons and, in their neutral state, **17 electrons**. 

Because they have the same number of electrons, they also have the **same electron configuration**: 
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^5$$

highlights.text: both isotopes have the same number of electrons and the same electron configuration

steps.title: Conclude identical chemical behavior
steps.content: Because their valence shells are identical, they will form the same types of bonds and react in exactly the same way with other substances. Therefore, their chemical properties are almost identical.

parts.content: Define the term *isotope*.
parts.markscheme:
- Define isotopes as atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the chemical identity
steps.content:
To define an isotope using the subatomic particle approach, we first look at what remains constant. For any given element, such as the technetium ($^{99\text{m}}\text{Tc}$) mentioned in the question, the identity of the atom is determined by its **atomic number**.

This means that all isotopes of the same element must have the **same number of protons**.

steps.title: Identify the mass difference
steps.content:
While the number of protons stays the same, isotopes differ in their **mass number**. Since the mass of an atom is essentially the sum of its protons and neutrons, and the proton count is fixed, the variation in mass must come from the other particle in the nucleus.

Therefore, isotopes contain a **different number of neutrons**.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
By combining these two observations, we get the standard IB definition required for the mark. This concept is found in **Topic S1.2.2 Isotopes** of the syllabus.

**Definition:** Isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

steps.title: Define Atomic Number (Z)
steps.content:
In chemistry, every element is uniquely identified by its **atomic number ($Z$)**. This number represents the total number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. 

Because all atoms of a specific element (like technetium) have the same number of protons, they all share the same atomic number ($Z$). This concept is covered in **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom**.

steps.title: Define Mass Number (A)
steps.content:
The **mass number ($A$)** is the sum of the protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus. While the number of protons defines the element, the number of neutrons can vary between atoms of that same element.

Mathematically, we can express this as:
$$A = Z + N$$
where $N$ is the number of neutrons.

steps.title: Combine for Isotope Definition
steps.content:
Using the atomic and mass number approach, we define **isotopes** as atoms of the same element that have the **same atomic number ($Z$)** but **different mass numbers ($A$)**.

This difference in mass numbers arises specifically because the atoms have a different number of neutrons in their nuclei. This answer satisfies the markscheme requirement by linking the chemical identity (same protons/$Z$) to the physical mass difference (different neutrons/mass number $A$).

parts.content: State the number of protons and neutrons in $^{99\text{m}}\text{Tc}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State that there are 43 protons and 56 neutrons (from $99 - 43 = 56$) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Award the mark only if both numbers are correct.</note>

steps.title: Identify the mass number
steps.content:
From the isotopic notation $^{99\text{m}}\text{Tc}$, we can identify the **mass number** ($A$). The superscript number represents the total number of nucleons (protons + neutrons) in the nucleus. 

$$A = 99$$

steps.title: Find the atomic number
steps.content:
Next, we look up Technetium ($\text{Tc}$) in the **Periodic Table**. The atomic number ($Z$) defines the element and tells us the number of protons in the nucleus.

Technetium has an atomic number of **43**.

$$\text{Number of protons} = 43$$

steps.title: Calculate number of neutrons
steps.content:
The number of neutrons is found by subtracting the atomic number from the mass number. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.2.1: Atomic Structure**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Number of neutrons} &= A - Z \\ &= 99 - 43 \\ &= 56 \end{aligned}$$

steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state both values correctly. 

**Protons:** 43  
**Neutrons:** 56

parts.markscheme:

- Explain that chemical properties depend on the arrangement or interactions of electrons, which are identical for isotopes of the same element <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Define isotopes and electrons
steps.content: To understand chemical properties, we first look at what makes an atom an isotope. Isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons (atomic number). In a neutral atom, the number of electrons must equal the number of protons. Therefore, all isotopes of the same element have the **same number of electrons**.

steps.title: State the electronic arrangement
steps.content:
Since isotopes have the same number of electrons, they also have the exact same **arrangement of electrons** (electronic configuration). This includes having the same number of valence electrons in their outer shell.

This point is crucial for your first mark ($A1$).

steps.title: Link electrons to chemistry
steps.content:
Chemical reactions are essentially interactions between the electrons of different atoms—specifically the losing, gaining, or sharing of valence electrons to form bonds. 

Because the number and arrangement of electrons are identical between isotopes, they behave the same way during these interactions. This is why their chemical properties are similar.

Stating that chemical properties depend on these electron interactions earns the second mark ($R1$).

- Explain that the isotope emits gamma radiation which can be detected by a scanner or gamma camera to produce an image of internal organs <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the medical use
steps.content:
In medicine, isotopes like $^{99\text{m}}\text{Tc}$ are primarily used for **medical imaging** or as a **radioactive tracer**. These isotopes help doctors visualize the structure and function of internal organs without the need for invasive surgery.

This concept is a key application of what you learn about isotopes in **Topic S1.2.2: Isotopes**.

highlights.text: Identify one use of isotopes in medicine

steps.title: Explain the mechanism
steps.content: The isotope works by emitting **gamma ($\gamma$) radiation** from inside the body. Gamma rays are high-energy electromagnetic waves that are highly penetrating, meaning they can pass through body tissues and exit the patient's body to be measured.

highlights.text: explain briefly how the isotope works

steps.title: Detection and imaging
steps.content: Once the gamma rays exit the body, they are detected by external sensors such as a **scanner** or a **gamma camera**. A computer then processes these signals to produce a detailed image of the target organ, allowing for a diagnosis.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, ensure you include both the use and the mechanism:

1.  **Identify use:** Medical imaging or radioactive tracer. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Explain mechanism:** It emits gamma radiation that passes through the body to be detected by an external scanner/camera to produce an image. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify medical use
steps.content: A major medical application of isotopes is **radiotherapy**, which is used for the treatment of cancer.

steps.title: Select specific isotope
steps.content:
Specific examples of isotopes used in radiotherapy include:
- **Iodine-131** ($^{131}\text{I}$): Used to treat thyroid cancer.
- **Cobalt-60** ($^{60}\text{Co}$): Used in external beam radiation therapy for various tumors.

steps.title: Explain ionizing radiation
steps.content: These isotopes work by emitting **ionizing radiation** (such as beta particles or gamma rays).

steps.title: Mechanism of action
steps.content: The ionizing radiation is targeted at cancerous cells, where it causes significant damage to their **DNA**. This damage prevents the cells from dividing and eventually leads to cell death, thereby shrinking the tumor.

steps.title: Locate carbon's atomic number
steps.content:
To find the number of electrons, we first look at the periodic table for the element carbon (C). Every element is defined by its **atomic number** ($Z$), which represents the number of protons in its nucleus. 

For any isotope of carbon, including carbon-14, the atomic number is **6**.

steps.title: Define a neutral atom
steps.content:
The question specifies that we are looking for the number of electrons in a **neutral atom**. This is a key concept in **Topic S1.2.1: Atomic Structure**. 

In a neutral atom, the total positive charge from the protons must be perfectly balanced by the total negative charge from the electrons. Therefore:

$$\text{Number of protons} = \text{Number of electrons}$$

steps.title: Determine the electron count
steps.content:
Since we identified that the atomic number of carbon is 6, we know it has 6 protons. For the atom to be neutral, it must also have exactly 6 electrons.

Note: The "14" in carbon-14 refers to the **mass number** (protons + neutrons). While carbon-14 has more neutrons than the common carbon-12, the number of electrons remains the same for all neutral isotopes of the same element.

$$\text{Electrons} = 6$$

steps.title: Identify protons and electrons
steps.content:
To compare isotopes, we first look at the atomic number ($Z$). Carbon is the 6th element in the periodic table, meaning its atomic number is 6. Since both are neutral atoms of carbon, they both contain **6 protons** and **6 electrons**.

This fundamental similarity is why isotopes of the same element behave identically in chemical reactions. This discovery is covered in **Structure 1.2: The nuclear atom**.

steps.title: Calculate neutrons for Carbon-12
steps.content:
Next, we find the number of neutrons by subtracting the atomic number ($Z = 6$) from the mass number ($A$). For carbon-12:

$$\text{Neutrons} = A - Z$$
$$\text{Neutrons} = 12 - 6 = 6$$

So, carbon-12 has **6 neutrons**.

steps.title: Calculate neutrons for Carbon-14
steps.content:
Now we do the same for carbon-14. Using its mass number of 14:

$$\text{Neutrons} = 14 - 6 = 8$$

Therefore, carbon-14 has **8 neutrons**.

To earn the full 2 marks, you must explicitly state both the similarities and the differences:

1. Both isotopes have **6 protons and 6 electrons** (1 mark).
2. Carbon-14 has **8 neutrons**, while carbon-12 has **6 neutrons** (1 mark).

parts.content: State what is represented by the x-axis labeled "$m/z$".
parts.markscheme:
Mass-to-charge ratio of the ion (typically mass divided by charge, where charge is usually $+1$).
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the axis label
steps.content: In a mass spectrum, the horizontal axis (x-axis) provides information about the particles being detected. The label $m/z$ is a standard notation used in mass spectrometry, which you can find discussed in **Topic S1.2.3: Mass Spectrometry**.

steps.title: Define the components
steps.content:
The symbol $m/z$ is defined as the **mass-to-charge ratio** of an ion. 

- $m$ represents the relative atomic or molecular mass of the ion.
- $z$ represents the numerical charge of the ion.

steps.title: State the technical definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state that it represents the **mass-to-charge ratio**. 

In most undergraduate and IB-level problems, the ions formed have a charge of $+1$ ($z = 1$). This means the $m/z$ value on the x-axis usually corresponds directly to the mass of the ion.

parts.content: Identify the species responsible for the peak at $m/z=18$.
parts.markscheme:
$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$ (molecular ion of water).
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the target peak
steps.content: In mass spectrometry, the x-axis represents the **mass-to-charge ratio** ($m/z$). Since the charge ($z$) is almost always $+1$ for these types of questions, the $m/z$ value directly corresponds to the mass of the detected species. We are looking for the species with a mass of approximately 18.

steps.title: Calculate the molecular mass
steps.content:
We are given that the substance is water, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. To find its relative molecular mass ($M_r$), we sum the relative atomic masses of its constituent atoms from the periodic table:

- Relative atomic mass of Hydrogen (H): $\approx 1.01$
- Relative atomic mass of Oxygen (O): $\approx 16.00$

$$\begin{aligned} M_r(\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= (2 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ M_r(\text{H}_2\text{O}) &= 18.02 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation confirms that the peak at $m/z=18$ represents the entire water molecule.

gdc.instructions: In the calculation menu, type 2 * 1.01 + 16.00 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 1.01 + 16.00 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * 1.01 + 16.00 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the relative molecular mass of water.

steps.title: Identify the ionized species
steps.content:
Mass spectrometers only detect **ions**. When the water molecule is hit by high-energy electrons in the ionization chamber, it loses an electron to become a positively charged **molecular ion**. 

Therefore, the species responsible for the peak is the molecular ion of water, written as $\text{H}_2\text{O}^+$.

steps.title: Calculate the molecular mass
steps.content:
To identify the species at a specific $m/z$ value, we first determine the relative molecular mass ($M_r$) of the substance being analyzed. For water ($H_2O$):

$$\text{Molar mass of } H_2O = (2 \times 1.01) + 16.00 = 18.02$$

In mass spectrometry, we typically round this to the nearest whole number for identification, which is 18.

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly.

desmos.instructions: Enter the calculation.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of water.

steps.title: Identify the parent peak
steps.content:
In a mass spectrum, the peak corresponding to the relative molecular mass of the sample is known as the **parent peak** or the **molecular ion peak** ($M^+$). 

Since the molar mass of water is 18, the peak at $m/z = 18$ represents the intact water molecule that has been ionized by losing exactly one electron. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom**.

steps.title: State the species
steps.content:
The mass spectrometer detects positive ions. When the water molecule is ionized, it forms the molecular ion:

$$H_2O \rightarrow H_2O^+ + e^-$$

Therefore, the species responsible for the peak at $m/z = 18$ is the water molecular ion, written as $H_2O^+$.

parts.content: Suggest the likely species responsible for the peaks at $m/z=17$ and $16$.
parts.markscheme:
- $m/z = 17$: likely $\mathrm{OH}^+$ ion (fragment of water). <marks>1 mark</marks>

- $m/z = 16$: likely $\mathrm{O}^+$ ion (further fragmentation of water molecule). <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the parent ion
steps.content:
In mass spectrometry, the peak with the highest $m/z$ value (ignoring small isotope peaks) usually represents the **molecular ion** ($M^+$). For water ($H_2O$), the molecular mass is approximately $18.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$.

Identifying the parent ion at $m/z = 18$ tells us the starting point for our subtractive analysis:
$$H_2O + e^- \rightarrow H_2O^+ + 2e^-$$

This $H_2O^+$ ion has a mass of 18.

steps.title: Analyze peak m/z = 17
steps.content:
Using the **Subtractive Fragmentation Analysis** approach, we calculate the mass lost from the parent ion to reach the fragment peak at $m/z = 17$.

$$\text{Mass lost} = 18 - 17 = 1$$

A loss of 1 mass unit corresponds to the loss of one **hydrogen atom** ($H$). Subtracting one hydrogen from the water molecule ($H_2O - H$) leaves us with an $OH$ fragment. Since the mass spectrometer only detects positively charged species, the likely ion is:

$$\text{Species} = OH^+$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 18 minus 17.

ti84.instructions: Enter 18 - 17 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 18 - 17 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass difference for the first fragment.

steps.title: Analyze peak m/z = 16
steps.content:
Next, we perform the same subtractive calculation for the peak at $m/z = 16$.

$$\text{Mass lost} = 18 - 16 = 2$$

A loss of 2 mass units corresponds to the loss of **two hydrogen atoms** ($2H$). Subtracting two hydrogens from the water molecule ($H_2O - 2H$) leaves us with just the oxygen atom. 

The likely ion responsible for this peak is:

$$\text{Species} = O^+$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 18 minus 16.

ti84.instructions: Enter 18 - 16 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 18 - 16 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass difference for the second fragment.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
By identifying the mass lost relative to the parent ion ($m/z = 18$), we determine the identity of the fragments. 

*   For $m/z = 17$, the species is **$OH^+$** (1 mark).
*   For $m/z = 16$, the species is **$O^+$** (1 mark).

steps.title: Identify molecular components
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Mass Composition Matching** approach, we first look at the elements in the parent molecule, water ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$). We know the approximate atomic masses for the main isotopes are:
- Oxygen ($\mathrm{O}$): $16$
- Hydrogen ($\mathrm{H}$): $1$

In mass spectrometry, peaks represent ions that have been formed by the parent molecule fragmenting as it is ionized.

steps.title: Match the peak at 17
steps.content:
We need to find a combination of oxygen and hydrogen atoms that sums to a mass of $17$. 

$$\text{Mass of } (\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{H}) = 16 + 1 = 17$$

This fragment corresponds to the loss of one hydrogen atom from the water molecule. Since mass spectrometry only detects positive ions, the species is the hydroxide ion fragment:

$$m/z = 17 \rightarrow \mathrm{OH}^+$$

gdc.instructions: Type 16 + 1 in the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 16 + 1 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 16 + 1 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Verify the sum for the OH fragment mass.

steps.title: Match the peak at 16
steps.content:
Next, we look for a combination that sums to a mass of $16$. 

$$\text{Mass of } (\mathrm{O}) = 16$$

This matches a single oxygen atom. This occurs when the water molecule loses both hydrogen atoms. Representing it as a positive ion, we identify it as:

$$m/z = 16 \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^+$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 16.

ti84.instructions: Press 16 and ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 16.

calculator.description: Confirm the mass of the oxygen atom.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
The likely species responsible for the fragments are:
- **$m/z = 17$**: $\mathrm{OH}^+$
- **$m/z = 16$**: $\mathrm{O}^+$

You would earn one mark for each correct ion identification.

parts.content: Explain why the peak at $m/z=18$ is the most intense.
parts.markscheme:
The molecular ion ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$) is the most abundant because it is the original water molecule ionized but not broken apart.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Determine the molar mass
steps.content:
First, let's calculate the relative molecular mass ($M_r$) of water, $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$. Using the relative atomic masses from the periodic table:

$$\begin{aligned} M_r(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}) &= (2 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &= 18.02 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms that the peak at $m/z = 18$ corresponds to the mass of the entire water molecule. This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic 1.4: Molar mass**.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses: 2 * 1.01 + 16.00

desmos.instructions: Enter the sum of the atomic masses: 2 * 1.01 + 16.00

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of water.

steps.title: Identify the molecular ion
steps.content:
In mass spectrometry, the peak corresponding to the mass of the original molecule is called the **molecular ion** ($M^+$). It is formed when a water molecule loses a single electron but does not break apart:

$$\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} + e^- \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+ + 2e^-$$

Because the charge ($z$) is $+1$, the $m/z$ value is simply equal to the mass of the ion, which is 18.

steps.title: Explain peak intensity
steps.content:
The intensity (height) of a peak in a mass spectrum represents the **relative abundance** of that specific ion. 

The peak at $m/z=18$ is the most intense because the $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$ ion is the most abundant species. This happens because it is the original water molecule that has been ionized but has not yet undergone **fragmentation** (breaking into smaller pieces like $\mathrm{OH}^+$ or $\mathrm{O}^+$).

highlights.text: peak at m/z=18 is the most intense

steps.title: Final summary
steps.content:
To get the mark on the exam, you need to state that $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$ is the molecular ion and that it remains intact. 

**Final Answer:** The molecular ion ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$) is the most abundant because it is the original water molecule that has been ionized but not broken apart.

steps.title: Identify the molecular ion
steps.content:
In mass spectrometry, the peak with an $m/z$ value corresponding to the total relative molecular mass of the compound is called the **molecular ion** ($M^+$). 

For water ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$), we can calculate the mass of the unfragmented molecule:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Mass} &= (2 \times \text{Mass of H}) + (1 \times \text{Mass of O}) \\ &= (2 \times 1.01) + 16.00 \\ &\approx 18 \end{aligned}$$

This peak represents the whole water molecule that has lost one electron but has not broken apart.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * 1.01) + 16.00 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (2 * 1.01) + 16.00 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the relative molecular mass of water to confirm the peak identity.

steps.title: Compare fragmentation vs. ionization
steps.content:
When water enters the mass spectrometer, it is bombarded with high-energy electrons. This energy can lead to two outcomes:
1. **Ionization**: The molecule loses an electron to become $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$ ($m/z = 18$).
2. **Fragmentation**: The molecule breaks apart into smaller pieces, such as $\mathrm{OH}^+$ ($m/z = 17$) or $\mathrm{H}^+$ ($m/z = 1$).

While fragmentation does occur (which explains the smaller peaks we see), it is a less frequent event than simple ionization.

steps.title: Explain peak intensity
steps.content:
The **intensity** of a peak on the y-axis represents the relative abundance of that ion hitting the detector. 

Because the majority of water molecules remain whole during the ionization process rather than breaking into fragments, the unfragmented molecular ion ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^+$) is the most abundant species. Consequently, it produces the most intense peak at $m/z = 18$.

parts.content: Predict what effect the presence of a small amount of heavy water ($\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$, where $\mathrm{D} = {}^{2}\mathrm{H}$) would have on the mass spectrum.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$ would produce an additional peak at $m/z = 20$ ($\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}^+$) and possibly at $m/z = 19$ for $\mathrm{HDO}^+$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- These peaks would be much smaller in intensity compared to $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ peaks if only a small amount of heavy water is present. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify new isotopic species
steps.content:
To predict the effect on the mass spectrum, we first identify the new molecular species (isotopologues) formed when deuterium ($D$ or ${}^{2}\mathrm{H}$) is present. Since we have a mixture of normal water and heavy water, we can expect to see ions formed from both $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$ and potentially $\mathrm{HDO}$.

This concept is part of **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom**, specifically focusing on how isotopes affect molecular mass in mass spectrometry.

steps.title: Calculate m/z for D2O
steps.content:
We calculate the mass-to-charge ratio ($m/z$) for the heavy water parent ion, $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}^{+}$. 

Given that $\mathrm{D}$ has a mass of 2 and $\mathrm{O}$ has a mass of 16:

$$\text{Mass of } \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O} = (2 \times 2) + 16 = 20$$

Therefore, a new peak will appear at $m/z = 20$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2*2 + 16 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 2*2 + 16 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 2*2 + 16 in the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of D2O.

steps.title: Account for HDO formation
steps.content:
In a mixture of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$, hydrogen atoms can exchange to form $\mathrm{HDO}$. We calculate the $m/z$ for the $\mathrm{HDO}^{+}$ ion:

$$\text{Mass of } \mathrm{HDO} = 1 + 2 + 16 = 19$$

This would result in an additional peak at $m/z = 19$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 + 2 + 16 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 1 + 2 + 16 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1 + 2 + 16 in the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of HDO.

steps.title: Predict relative peak intensity
steps.content:
The relative intensity of peaks in a mass spectrum corresponds to the abundance of the ions. Because the question specifies there is only a "small amount" of heavy water, the number of $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{HDO}^{+}$ ions reaching the detector will be much lower than the number of $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}^{+}$ ions.

Consequently, the new peaks at $m/z = 20$ and $m/z = 19$ will have a **very low relative intensity** (they will be much shorter) compared to the main peak at $m/z = 18$.
